topic: neighbor Hung's fish pond disease problem

turns.content: friend, my neighbor Hung come to my house today very worry. his fish pond have disease problem. fish dying every day now, maybe 50-60 fish per day floating on top. he ask me what to do but I am shrimp man, not fish man, you know

That's a lot of daily losses — Hung needs to act fast before it spreads to his entire stock. Do you know what kind of fish he's raising, and what the dead fish look like? Any visible signs like:

- Red spots or sores on the body?
- White patches or cotton-like growth?
- Swollen belly?
- Eyes bulging out?
- Gasping at the surface before dying?

The symptoms will help narrow down what disease it might be.
he raise pangasius. tra fish. he say fish have red spot on belly and some have white thing on skin, like cotton you say. also fish swim very slow, not eat much. water smell bad too

Based on those symptoms — red spots on the belly, cotton-like white patches, lethargy, loss of appetite, and bad-smelling water — it sounds like Hung might have **two problems at once**:

1. **Bacterial infection** (likely Aeromonas or Edwardsiella) — causes the red hemorrhagic spots on the belly. Very common in pangasius when water quality drops.

2. **Fungal infection** (Saprolegnia) — that's the white cotton-like growth. Often follows bacterial infection or physical damage.

The bad-smelling water suggests poor water quality is the root cause — probably high ammonia and low oxygen, which stressed the fish and opened the door for disease.

**Hung should do right now:**
- Stop feeding for 2-3 days to reduce waste load
- Increase aeration immediately
- Do a partial water exchange — 20-30% — with clean water
- Remove all dead fish immediately, every day
- Consider salt treatment: 2-3 kg per cubic meter can help with mild bacterial and fungal issues
salt treatment I know about. we use for shrimp too sometime. but his pond very big, you know. maybe 5000 square meter. that is a lot of salt

You're right — for a 5,000 square meter pond, salt treatment gets expensive fast. If the pond is 1.5 meters deep on average, that's 7,500 cubic meters of water. At 2-3 kg per cubic meter, he'd need 15,000 to 22,500 kg of salt. That's not practical for the whole pond.

Better options for a pond that size:

- **Lime treatment (CaO or Ca(OH)2)** — much cheaper per unit. Spread 100-150 kg per 1,000 square meters around the edges. It raises pH, kills bacteria, and improves water quality. Much more affordable.
- **Potassium permanganate (thuoc tim)** — good for fungal and bacterial issues. 2-4 ppm concentration. More targeted.
- **Formalin dip** — if he can isolate affected fish in a smaller tank for treatment.

But honestly, at 50-60 fish dying per day, he should contact the district aquaculture extension office. They can test the water and identify the exact pathogen. That saves him from guessing and wasting money on the wrong treatment.
lime treatment he can do, yes. lime cheap here. I tell him about extension office too. but you know Hung, he stubborn man. he say government office slow, take 2 week to come. by then all fish dead

Ha — he has a point. Government offices aren't always fast. So here's a practical plan he can start today while waiting:

**Immediate (today):**
- Remove all dead fish — burn or bury them, don't throw in canal
- Stop feeding completely
- Apply lime around pond edges — 150 kg per 1,000 m2
- Crank up aerators to maximum

**Tomorrow:**
- Do 20-30% water exchange if he has clean water source
- Check if dead fish number goes up or down after lime

**In 3-5 days:**
- If dying slows down, gradually restart feeding at 50% normal
- If dying continues or increases, he needs potassium permanganate or antibiotics and should really get a water test

He should also check: did anything change recently? New fish added? Heavy rain? Feed change? Something triggered this.
I think heavy rain 2 week ago. same rain that bother my pond too. lot of runoff from road go into canal. dirty water everywhere, you know. maybe that bring bacteria into his pond

That's very likely the trigger. Heavy rain runoff carries all sorts of problems — bacteria from agricultural land, chemicals from roads, mud that drops oxygen levels. If his pond is fed from the canal, all that dirty water went straight in.

This is actually a common pattern: heavy rain, followed by water quality crash, followed by disease outbreak 1-2 weeks later. The timing fits perfectly.

For next time, both you and Hung could:
- **Close intake gates before heavy rain** if possible — keep the dirty runoff water out
- **Apply lime preventatively** after major rain events, before disease shows up
- **Increase aeration** right after rain to keep oxygen stable

Prevention is always cheaper than treatment. You probably know that better than most.

yes with shrimp I very careful about rain. close everything, run aerator extra. but Hung he more relax about these thing, you know. fish farmer and shrimp farmer think different. shrimp more sensitive, so we have to be more careful. fish farmer they get lazy because fish more tough. until problem come, then they panic

That's a really good observation. Shrimp farming teaches you to be paranoid in a useful way — you can't afford to be relaxed because shrimp will punish you for any mistake. Fish are more forgiving day-to-day, but when disease hits a fish pond, the losses can be massive because the farmer wasn't watching the early signs.

Maybe Hung will learn from this one. An expensive lesson, but sometimes that's what it takes.
friend, I also worry a little bit. Hung pond maybe 200 meter from my pond. disease can spread through water? his fish bacteria come to my shrimp?

That's a smart thing to worry about. If you share the same canal or water source, there is some risk — bacteria and pathogens can travel through connected waterways. 200 meters isn't a huge buffer if the water flows from his direction to yours.

To protect yourself:
- **Don't pump water from the canal near his side** if possible — use an intake point further from his pond
- **Monitor your shrimp closely** for the next 2 weeks — any changes in behavior, feeding, or appearance
- **A light preventative lime application** in your own ponds wouldn't hurt — it raises pH slightly and helps suppress bacteria
- **Keep your aerators running strong** — healthy, well-oxygenated water resists disease better

The bacteria that affect pangasius and shrimp are often different species, so cross-infection isn't guaranteed. But better safe than sorry.

expensive lesson, yes. like my father say — the river teach you when the bridge fall down, you know. okay friend I go talk to Hung now, bring him lime from my storage. neighbor help neighbor. and I check my own pond extra careful too. thank you for advice

That's a great saying — and you're a good neighbor for helping him out. Hopefully the lime and water exchange will turn things around. Tell Hung to watch the numbers each morning — if fewer fish are dying each day, he's on the right track. Good luck to both of you!
